coredata.dc:title: Simultaneous regulation of pore size and surface charge of nanofiltration membrane using carbon quantum dots for improved selective separation
coredata.dc:creator: Mi, Yi-Fang
Huang, Yi-Han
He, Shu-Heng
Ma, Rong
Meng, Yi-Ding
Cao, Zhi-Hai
coredata.dc:description: Designing nanofiltration (NF) membranes with tunable pore sizes and surface charges is highly desirable to meet the growing demand for precise separation. In this study, carbon quantum dots generated through lysine pyrolysis (l-CQDs) were utilized as monomers to fabricate NF membranes via interfacial polymerization (IP). Intriguingly, the surface charge (from negative to positive) and effective pore size (from loose to dense structure) of the resultant NF membranes could be simultaneously modulated by simply adjusting the diffusion of l-CQDs during the IP process, which endowed the obtained l-CQD-based NF membrane with a tunable separation performance. The negative and loose NF membranes (l-CQD; 0.1 wt%) achieved excellent dye/salt fractionation performance even at high NaCl concentrations (20 g/L). The recovered NaCl from the highly saline dyeing effluent could be directly reused in cotton fabric dyeing, indicating potential application prospects in the treatment of saline textile effluents. A dense and positive NF membrane was obtained when l-CQD concentration increased to 1.0 wt%; this membrane showed exceptional desalination performance with high rejection of MgCl2, CuCl2, and CaCl2 (98.77 %, 99.06 %, and 99.0 %, respectively), demonstrating the ability to remove metal ions from water. This work provides an approach for preparing NF membranes with tailorable pore size and surface charge and highlights the potential of l-CQD-based NF membranes for the practical separation toward various charged components.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanofiltration membrane
Reversal of surface charge
Tunable effective pore size
Carbon quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Reuse of NaCl from dyeing effluent for cotton fabric dyeing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the fabrication process of NF-CQD membranes via interfacial polymerization.

(a) Optical photographs of l-CQD aqueous solution in contact with TMC/hexane solution in a glass beaker. (b) ATR-FITR spectra, (c) XPS spectra, and (d) high-resolution O1s spectra of NF-CQD membranes.
SEM and AFM images of NF-CQD membranes.
(a) Water contact angle and surface free energy and (b) zeta potential of NF-CQD membranes (Inset: the IEP of the characterized membranes).
(a) Pore size distributions of NF-CQD 1, NF-CQD 8, and NF-CQD 10. (Inset: the corresponding MWCO of the characterized membranes); (b) effect of l-CQD concentrations on the separation performances.

(a) Effect of operation pressure on the separation performance of NF-CQD 2, NF-CQD 8, and NF-CQD 10; (b) antifouling performance of NF-CQD 2 and NF-CQD 8 (test condition: 0.1 g/L BSA aqueous solution); (c) effect of MgCl2 concentration on the separation performance of NF-CQD 10.

(a) Solute rejections of NF-CQD 1; (b) effect of NaCl salt concentration (1–20 g/L NaCl solution, 0.1 g/L CR solution) on the NF performance of NF-CQD 1; (c) apparent colors and (d) colorimetric parameters of the two dyed cotton fabrics when using the recovered saline water and freshwater, respectively.

head.ce:title: Simultaneous regulation of pore size and surface charge of nanofiltration membrane using carbon quantum dots for improved selective separation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Advanced Textile Materials and Manufacturing Technology and Engineering Research Center for Eco-Dyeing & Finishing of Textiles

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Designing nanofiltration (NF) membranes with tunable pore sizes and surface charges is highly desirable to meet the growing demand for precise separation. In this study, carbon quantum dots generated through lysine pyrolysis (l-CQDs) were utilized as monomers to fabricate NF membranes via interfacial polymerization (IP). Intriguingly, the surface charge (from negative to positive) and effective pore size (from loose to dense structure) of the resultant NF membranes could be simultaneously modulated by simply adjusting the diffusion of l-CQDs during the IP process, which endowed the obtained l-CQD-based NF membrane with a tunable separation performance. The negative and loose NF membranes (l-CQD; 0.1 wt%) achieved excellent dye/salt fractionation performance even at high NaCl concentrations (20 g/L). The recovered NaCl from the highly saline dyeing effluent could be directly reused in cotton fabric dyeing, indicating potential application prospects in the treatment of saline textile effluents. A dense and positive NF membrane was obtained when l-CQD concentration increased to 1.0 wt%; this membrane showed exceptional desalination performance with high rejection of MgCl2, CuCl2, and CaCl2 (98.77 %, 99.06 %, and 99.0 %, respectively), demonstrating the ability to remove metal ions from water. This work provides an approach for preparing NF membranes with tailorable pore size and surface charge and highlights the potential of l-CQD-based NF membranes for the practical separation toward various charged components.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: The escalating global water scarcity is regarded as one of the greatest crises, and it threatens human health [1]. Membrane separations have aroused interest owing to their high efficacy and low energy consumption in sustainable water resource management for supplying adequate safe and clean water [2,3]. Nanofiltration (NF) membranes can efficiently separate multivalent ions and small organic molecules and can therefore be used in the fields of desalination, municipal and industrial wastewater treatment, resource recovery, and material separation and concentration [4-7]. The dominant advantage of NF membranes over ultrafiltration and reverse osmosis membranes is high selectivity [8]; therefore, sustaining improved NF membrane separation performance, especially selectivity, is highly desirable to fully meet the growing demand for separation.

The separation mechanism of NF membranes is primarily governed by steric hindrance and the Donnan effect [9,10]. For example, dye/salt separation NF membranes usually possess a loose separation layer structure. Desalination NF membranes have dense selective layers with relevant surface charges, that is, positive surface charges for Mg2+, Ca2+, and other heavy metal ion removal and negative surface charges for multivalent anion removal. This has triggered growing interest in simultaneously tailoring the pore size and surface charge characteristics of NF membranes. Many methods, such as layer-by-layer self-assembly [11] and deposition/coating [12,13], have been successfully implemented to adjust the pore size and/or surface charge of NF membranes. However, these methods suffer from issues of multi-step operation, leading to tedious processes [7,11], uncontrollable charges [14], relatively large effective pore sizes [15], and high costs [7,11].

In comparison, interfacial polymerization (IP) is a more stable and practical method to prepare state-of-the-art NF membranes [16-18]. To date, polyamide NF membranes fabricated via IP process have dominated the water industry market [2]. The diamine monomers are commonly piperazine or polyethyleneimine, and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) is used as an acyl chloride monomer. Much work has been done to modulate the surface charge of NF membranes by surface grafting of target molecules through chemical reactions using pre-introduced or residual reactive groups on the membrane surface [19-22]. Nevertheless, the effectiveness of these two-step modifications is limited by the conditions of reactive groups on the surface, which require accurate control of the fabrication parameters. The pore sizes of NF membranes could be tuned simply by manipulating the IP conditions. By contrast, tailoring the membrane surface charge by manipulating IP conditions is difficult. It usually needs ultralow piperazine or polyethyleneimine concentrations [23,24], support-free or modified substrates [23,25], not to mention the harsh fabrication conditions; for instance, low/high two-phase temperature [26,27], additional reagents (amine monomers or surfactants) [9,28,29], and further post-treatment [30]. Considering the formation mechanism of IP, the reaction of the amine groups with TMC affects the pore size, and the residual amine groups can also affect the surface charge of the NF membrane, which is closely related to the structure of the monomer. Thus, it is feasible to manipulate the structure of monomers to simultaneously tune the surface charge and pore size of the NF membranes under mild IP conditions.

Nanosized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with the desired dispersity, distinguished chemical stability, biocompatibility, and easy fabrication properties have been adopted to improve membrane separation performance [31,32]. More importantly, its chemical structure can be easily altered by the variations of precursors, which will benefit the design of CQDs to realize simultaneously tune the surface charge and pore size of the NF membranes. Currently, CQDs acting as aqueous monomers [33,34], nanofillers [35], or intermediate layers [36] have been incorporated into polyamide NF membranes in most studies. Despite these efforts in improving the separation performance, membranes that simultaneously tailor their size and surface charge have rarely been explored.

Therefore, in this study, lysine was adopted as the precursor, lysine-based CQDs (l-CQDs) were rationally designed as building blocks, and their potential in the regulation of the surface charge and pore size of NF membranes via general IP was fully explored (Fig. 1 ). The assembled CQDs were covalently bonded, enabling the formation of a highly selective separation layer. By rationally designing CQDs with numerous amine groups and modulating their concentration over a wider range (0.1–1.0 wt%), the contents of carboxyl and amino groups can be adjusted to change the surface charge and even realize charge reversal of NF membranes. Therefore, NF membranes with diverse selectivities were obtained. The loose and negatively charged NF membranes (l-CQD; 0.1 wt%) achieved excellent dye/salt fractionation performance even at high NaCl concentrations (20 g/L). The recovered NaCl from the highly saline dyeing effluent could be directly reused in cotton fabric dyeing, indicating prominent application prospects in treating saline textile effluents. The separation layer with reduced pore size possessed positively charged surfaces when the l-CQD concentration increased to 1.0 wt%. The reduction in effective pore size and positive surface charge endowed the membrane with high rejections of 98.77 %, 99.06 %, and 99.0 % for MgCl2, CuCl2, and CaCl2, respectively, demonstrating its ability to remove metal ions from water.

l-lysine, TMC, methyl blue (MYB), Congo red (CR), methylene blue (MB), Victoria blue B (VBB), bovine serum albumin (BSA), and polyethylene glycol (PEG) with molecular weights of 200, 400, 600, 800, and 1000 Da, respectively, were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., ltd. Magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), and sodium chloride (NaCl) were obtained from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory. Commercial polysulfone ultrafiltration membranes (PSF, molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) = 35,000 Da) were purchased from the Hangzhou Development Centre of Water Treatment Technology. Home-made deionized (DI) water was used in all the experiments.

The l-CQDs were synthesized using a pyrolytic procedure (Fig. S1). 1 g of l-lysine powder was heated at 240 °C for 30 min. After cooling to ambient temperature, the residue was dissolved in 20 mL DI water and sonicated for 30 min. The l-CQD solution was then filtered through filter membranes (0.22 μm) and the resultant filtrate was further dialyzed with a dialysis bag (MWCO = 1 kDa). Solid l-CQDs were obtained via freeze-drying.

NF-CQD membranes were prepared via IP process. The l-CQDs were dispersed in DI water through ultrasonication for 30 min to form a homogeneous aqueous solution. The PSF membrane surface was immersed in the l-CQD aqueous solution for 2 min. Then, draining off the excess l-CQD solution, the surface was immersed in hexane solutions containing 0.2 wt% TMC for 1 min to generate the NF-CQD membrane. The NF-CQD membrane was thermally heated at 50 °C for 15 min and thoroughly rinsed with DI water. The concentrations of l-CQDs in aqueous solution were 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 0.8, and 1.0 wt%, and the corresponding membranes were termed NF-CQD 1, NF-CQD 2, NF-CQD 5, NF-CQD 8, and NF-CQD 10, respectively.

The morphology of the l-CQDs was characterized using a 200 kV high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEM-2100). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, BRUKER AXS) was used to record the crystal structure of the l-CQDs. A Vertex Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) was used to confirm the chemical structures of the l-CQDs. The chemical structures and elemental compositions of the NF-CQD membrane were assessed using Nicolet Aratar 370 attenuated total reflectance FTIR (ATR-FTIR) and Thermo Fisher Scientific X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), respectively. The membrane surface and cross-sectional morphologies were visualized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, GeminiSEM500), atomic force microscopy (AFM, XE-100E), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-1230). Water contact angles were evaluated using a drop contract-angle system (Kruess, Germany, sessile drop mode). The membrane surface free energy (–ΔGSL) was obtained as
(1)
- - Δ
G
SL
=
γ L
1 +
cos θ
SAD
,

where θ, γL, and SAD are the water contact angle, water surface tension at 25 °C, and surface area difference obtained from the AFM image, respectively. Membrane surface potentials were acquired using a SurPASS Anton Paar Zeta potential analyzer, and 0.001 M KCl was employed as the background electrolyte solution.

The perm-selectivity of the NF-CQD membranes was evaluated using cross-flow NF equipment under 0.6 MPa. The feed solutions were 1.0 g/L inorganic salt (Na2SO4, MgSO4, MgCl2, and NaCl) aqueous solutions or 0.1 g/L dye (MYB, CR, and VBB) solutions. The surface area of each test membrane was 22.5 cm2. After pre-compaction at 0.7 MPa for 1 h, the water flux (J) and salt rejection (R) were calculated using the following equations:
(2)
J
=
V
St
,
(3)
R
%
=
C f
-
C p
C f
× 100 ,
where S (m2) represents the effective area, V (L) is the volume of permeate over time interval t (h), and Cf and Cp are the concentrations of the feed and permeate solutions, respectively.

In addition, the MWCO and pore size distribution (details are shown in Supporting Information) of the as-prepared NF membranes were determined via filtrating PEG solutions (1.0 g/L), and the PEGs concentrations were measured using a Shimadzu total organic carbon analyzer.

The salt/ water permeation selectivity is identical to the ratio of B value (solute permeability coefficient) to A value (water permeability) according to the literature [37]. The A value of NF membrane is obtained from Eq. (4). (the osmotic pressure difference was neglected).
(4)
J w
= A Δ P ,

where
J w
is water flux (L m−2h−1), ΔP is applied pressure (bar).

By assuming the solute (inorganic salts) permeation through NF membrane is controlled by the diffusion driven by concentration gradients, the B value is calculated from Eq. (5)
(5)
B =
J v
C p
C f
-
C p
,

where
J v
is water flux (L m−2h−1) when tested with a salt solution. The higher B/A value indicated a higher salt/water permeation selectivity, representing a lower rejection of salt [37]

The antifouling performance was further demonstrated using cyclic fouling tests. After being pre-pressurized at 0.7 MPa to reach a steady state, the initial water flux J0 of the NF membrane was obtained by testing pure water for 5 h. Then, an aqueous solution composed of 0.1 g/L BSA was used as the feed solution, and the water flux (Jf) was tested for 5 h. After the first cycle process, the fouled membrane was regenerated using DI water for 2 h, and the water flux (J1) of the cleaned membrane was measured by testing pure water. The parameters, flux recovery ratio (FRR), and total flux decline ratio (FDR), were calculated using Eqs. (6)–(7) to estimate the antifouling performance.
(6)
F R R =
J 1
J 0
× 100 % ,
(7)
F D R =
(
J 0
-
J f
)
J 0
× 100 % ,
where J0 and Jf are the pure water flux before and after the fouling process, respectively, and J1 is the water flux of the cleaned membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reuse of NaCl from dyeing effluent for cotton fabric dyeing
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical cotton fabric dyeing process, cotton fabrics (2 g) were mixed well with 100 mL of direct dye (0.2 g/L CR in this work) solution, while a certain amount of NaCl (6 g/L) was added to the dye effluent to promote the adsorption of dyes by the cotton fabrics, and the dyeing process was accomplished at 95 °C for 60 min. Subsequently, the dyeing effluent was nano-filtrated using NF-CQD 1 to reclaim the NaCl from the dyeing effluent. The reused NaCl solution was directly utilized for dyeing the cotton fabric. For comparison, the parallel dyeing process was conducted using a fresh NaCl aqueous solution. The colorimetric parameters K/S, L*, a*, and b*, which were measured using a Datacolor800 spectrophotometer (under D65 illuminant), were utilized to characterize the color difference between the two dyed fabrics [38].

The average lateral diameter of the l-CQDs was approximately 2.1 nm with good dispersion (Fig. S2a). The broad bands centered at 3356 and 3290 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. S2c) and the NH2 (399.4 eV) peaks observed in the N1s spectrum (Fig. S2f) collectively revealed the amino groups on the l-CQDs [39,40], which would be responsible for IP by covalently bonding with acyl chloride to form a polyamide selective layer. It was confirmed that l-CQDs with abundant amino groups were successfully prepared.

Polycondensation was qualitatively examined via the rapid formation of a thin film at the two-phase interface in a glass beaker (Fig. 2a). The characteristic peak of the amide bond at 1654 cm−1 was observed and the intensity increased with the increase in l-CQD concentration, indicating the successful IP reaction between l-CQDs and TMC (Fig. 2b) [41]. In the XPS spectra (Fig. 2c), new peaks attributed to N appeared in all NF-CQD membranes compared with PSF, and its content increased from 3.53 % to 8.98 % with increasing l-CQD concentration. Moreover, in the N1s spectrum of NF-CQD 2 (Fig. 2c inset), the amide bond (NCO) was observed. Besides, graphite N, and –N+ belonging to l-CQDs could also be found [40,42]. These results further demonstrated the successful generation of a separation layer through IP reaction between l-CQDs and TMC. The degree of cross-linking was quantitatively confirmed from the O 1s spectra. The high-resolution XPS O1s spectra (Fig. 2d) can be deconvoluted into OCN and OCO peaks, confirming a typical polyamide structure [40]. With the increase in l-CQD concentration, the content of OCN increased from 30.56 % to 58.82 %, indicating the higher cross-linking extent of the resultant NF-CQD membranes [25]. The significant CO bond peak (534.8 eV) originating from carboxyl groups was only shown in NF-CQD 2 [43]. In contrast, the oxygen vacancies attributed to l-CQDs (537 eV) were only observed in NF-CQD 10 [44]. All these results indicated the successful preparation of NF-CQD membranes, and the cross-linking degree increased with increasing l-CQD concentration.

The surface properties of the NF-CQDs were found to be highly related to the l-CQD content. As shown in Fig. 3
, a porous surface of the PSF membrane was observed. After polymerization, intact surfaces with no apparent pores were observed in all NF-CQD membranes. Meanwhile, with increasing l-CQD content from 0.2 wt% to 1.0 wt%, the defect-free surface morphology changed from a crater-like protrusion to a smooth structure. The formation of an interfacial reaction between l-CQDs and TMC has been demonstrated to be a nanoparticle-assembly process stemming from the unique properties of l-CQDs, which possess a hydrophobic core and hydrophilic chemical groups on the surface [33]. This indicates that the initial intact separation layer is formed by an ordered assembly of multi-CQD aggregated nanoparticles crosslinked by TMC. The hydrophobic core of l-CQDs gives l-CQDs a higher affinity toward hexane [45]. At low l-CQD concentrations, the continuous diffusion of l-CQDs to hexane can hardly be stopped because of the loose structure of the separation layer, leading to protrusions. When the l-CQD concentration was further increased, a dense separation layer quickly formed. This bulkier layer acts as an obstruction; thus, the newly generated multi-CQD aggregated nanoparticles would stack next to the former oligomers previously formed by l-CQDs with TMC. Thus, a more uniform structure composed of nanoparticles was observed in NF-CQD 10. The cross-sectional images of the membranes are shown in Fig. S3, and a typical defect-free selective layer with a thickness of ∼49 nm is evident in the TEM images (Fig. S4).

In addition to the surface morphology, the variations in hydrophilicity and surface charge with l-CQD concentration are shown in Fig. 4 . The water contact angle decreased from 66.5° (PSF) to 43.2° (NF-CQD 8), demonstrating the enhanced wettability of the membrane surface. This was ascribed to the hydrophilic groups of l-CQDs and carboxyl groups on the membrane surfaces. Furthermore, the -ΔGSL, which was “surface area corrected” interfacial free energy, was utilized to better reflect the hydrophilicity of the surface [46]. As shown in Fig. 4a, even though the water contact angles of NF-CQD 10 increased, the NF-CQD membranes with different l-CQD concentrations showed a similar -ΔGSL value of approximately 93 mJ m−2, which is higher than that of the PSF. Thus, we inferred that the increased water contact angles of NF-CQD 10 might be caused by the smoother surface, according to the Wenzel model [47].

Most importantly, the concentration of the l-CQDs dictates the charge reversal of the NF membrane. The NF membranes were negatively charged when the concentration of l-CQDs was in the range of 0.1–0.5 wt%. The values became almost neutral (1.98 mV) at an l-CQD concentration of 0.8 wt%. Subsequently, the membrane exhibited a positive charge (4.14 mV) when the l-CQD concentration was 1.0 wt%. Meanwhile, the isoelectric point (IEP) of the NF-CQD membranes shifted from 3.23 to 8.21. The negative charge of the NF membrane originates from the hydrolysis of the residual acyl chloride groups. When the aqueous phase concentration of l-CQDs was low, there were insufficient l-CQDs at the interface to consume TMC in the interfacial polymerization process; thus, the amount of free carboxylic acid was higher [23,48]. The reversal of the surface charge at high l-CQD concentrations was mainly attributed to the following reasons. On the one hand, the increase in l-CQD concentration improved the degree of reaction between l-CQDs and TMC (Fig. 2d), which led to a reduction in the number of unreacted carboxyl groups. On the other hand, the prepared l-CQDs were highly positively charged in the pH range of 3–10 (Fig. S5), and more positive groups could be incorporated into the separation layer [23]. As a result, it was firmly demonstrated that tuning the concentration of l-CQDs could efficiently change the surface charge property of NF membranes and even realize the reversal of surface charge without additional post-treatments, such as surface modification or secondary interfacial polymerization. The low positive zeta value of the NF membrane was sufficient to alter the rejection of ionic solutes, as reported in the literature [23,49]. Thus, we can infer that the facile tunable surface charge of our NF-CQD membranes will be beneficial for adjusting the separation performance of negatively or positively charged solutes, thus broadening their applications.

In addition to the surface properties, the pore sizes of the NF-CQD membranes were highly related to the l-CQD concentration. As shown in Fig. 5 a, PEGs with different molecular weights were used to determine the MWCO and pore size distributions of the NF-CQD membranes. The NF-CQD 1 membrane had an MWCO of ∼1992 Da, an effective pore size radius of 0.649 nm, and a wide pore size distribution. In contrast, NF-CQD 8 and NF-CQD 10 exhibited a decreased MWCO (587 Da and 384 Da, respectively) with narrow pore size distributions, and the effective pore size radii of NF-CQD 8 and NF-CQD 10 decreased to 0.283 and 0.263 nm, respectively. When the concentration of l-CQDs was low (0.1 wt%), the diffused l-CQDs could not consume all acyl chloride groups, resulting in looser separate layers with larger pore sizes [49]. Then, the acyl chloride groups on TMC molecules would thoroughly react with the diffused l-CQDs at higher concentrations, manifesting a higher cross-linking extent (Fig. 2d) and smaller pore sizes. It is evident that the charge properties and pore size can be modulated by adjusting the l-CQD concentration. Given the steric hindrance and Donnan effect, the resultant NF membranes exhibit attractive separation behavior with variations in l-CQD concentration.

Thus, the effects of the l-CQDs on salt rejection were studied under 0.6 MPa. In Fig. 5b, the pure water flux for NF-CQD 1 was 81.3 L m−2h−1, which was substantially reduced to 17.4 L m−2h−1 at a concentration of 1.0 wt%. Because there was no considerable difference in surface hydrophilicity (all membranes had a similar -ΔGSL) (Fig. 4a), the surface properties were not the key factor influencing pure water permeation. The water permeations were expected to be due to the different structural properties of the membranes, that is, higher hydraulic resistances (Fig.S6) and a smaller membrane pore size, which was ascribed to the higher cross-linking degree of the separation layers (Fig. 2d). Meanwhile, the salt rejection gradually increased but showed an interesting change. NF-CQD 1 exhibited low rejection of the four salts, indicating the separation characteristics of loose NF membranes. Both NF-CQD 2 and NF-CQD 5 showed typical negative charged NF membrane rejection behavior for inorganic salts [14], which was consistent with the Zeta potential test result. For NF-CQD 2, even though the Na+ radius was smaller than Mg2+ [50], the rejection of NaCl was still higher than that of MgCl2, verifying the dominant role of the Donnan effect in NF-CQD 2. The steric hindrance and Donnan effect both dominated the salt rejections behaviors of NF-CQD 5. This was because the NF-CQD 5 possessed a higher cross-linking degree compared to NF-CQD 2, which resulted in a smaller pore size. Further increasing the concentrations of l-CQDs, the reversal of NF membrane surface charge was realized. Specifically, the MgCl2 rejections of NF-CQD 8 and NF-CQD 10 were 90.94 % and 98.77 %, respectively. This was in sharp contrast to the relatively low MgCl2 rejections of NF-CQD 2 (10.09 %) and NF-CQD 5 (76.49 %). Due to the dense selective layer and almost neutral surface charge (1.98 mV), NF-CQD 8 exhibited a rejection sequence of MgCl2 ≈ Na2SO4 ≈ MgSO4 > NaCl, which rejected multivalent ions (regardless of anions and cations) more efficiently than monovalent ion [21]. NF-CQD 10 showed the rejection of salts in the order: MgCl2 > MgSO4 > Na2SO4 > NaCl, which was in line with the rejection behaviors of positive NF membranes [16]. The positive charge on the membrane surface contributed to the rejection of divalent cations. Meanwhile, the hydrated ionic radii of Mg2+ (0.428 nm) and SO4 2− (0.382 nm) were both larger than the effective membrane pore sizes (0.263 nm); thus, the enhanced electrostatic interactions (Fig. 2e) and steric exclusion of NF-CQD 10 contributed to high bivalent salt rejections [21]. Overall, the dominant feature of our work is the use of l-CQDs for membrane preparation to simultaneously modulate the membrane charge and pore size. By varying the concentration of l-CQDs, loose or dense NF membranes were obtained, as well as negatively or positively charged NF membranes (Fig. 4b).

Furthermore, the salt/water permeation selectivity of NF-CQD 2–10 was shown in Fig. S7. For negatively charged NF-CQD 2 and 5, Na2SO4/water permeation selectivity was calculated, while MgCl2/water permeation selectivity was calculated for NF-CQD 8 and 10. The higher salt/water permeation selectivity represented a lower rejection of salt [37]. For both Na2SO4 and MgCl2, it was observed that the B/A value decreased with the increase of l-CQD concentration. The result further indicated the regulation of l-CQDs concentration could simultaneously change the pore size and surface charge of NF membrane to achieve increased salt/water permeation selectivity.

The effects of operating pressure on the pure water flux of NF-CQD 2, NF-CQD 8, and NF-CQD 10 were investigated (Fig. 6 a). With operating pressure ranging from 0.3 to 0.6 MPa, the water flux of these NF membranes increased linearly. The pure water flux of NF-CQD 2, NF-CQD 8, and NF-CQD 10 is 37.19, 24.61, and 17.38 L m−2h−1 at 0.6 MPa, respectively. The linearly increasing pure water flux indicates that the pores of the membranes are not compacted in this pressure range, demonstrating the possibility of steady membrane operation [3].

Antifouling ability is crucial for NF membranes. Negatively charged BSA was chosen to assess the antifouling performance of NF-CQD 2 and NF-CQD 8. The normalized water flux of both membranes was reduced by BSA (Fig. 6b). After running in BSA solution for 2.5 cycles, the FDR and FRR values of NF-CQD 2 were 12.81 % and 95.62 %, while the FDR and FRR values of NF-CQD 8 were 14.94 % and 93.33 %, respectively. The lower FDR value and higher FRR value demonstrated better anti-fouling performance. For NF-CQD 2, strong electrostatic repulsion existed between the membrane surface and BSA foulants [51]. This repulsion could prevent BSA from contacting and firmly adsorbing on NF-CQD 2, therefore, water flux decreased by only 12.81 % and the deposited foulants could be washed away to regenerate the water flux. Though NF-CQD 8 was slightly positively charged, it still exhibited good antifouling performance, with a high FRR of 93.33 %. The enhanced antifouling performance might be due to the narrower pore size distribution and lower surface charge. The larger membrane pores have a higher local flux, leading to more serious concentration polarization and fouling accumulation phenomenon [52]. On the contrary, the flux is evenly distributed on the NF-CQD 8 membrane surface owing to its small pore sizes and narrower pore size distribution; thus, good antifouling performance was observed [53].

NF-CQD 10 is a positively charged NF membrane. When filtrated with 1 g/L inorganic salt solution at 0.6 MPa, the rejections to MgCl2, CuCl2, and CaCl2 were 98.77 %, 99.06 %, and 99 %, respectively, demonstrating the potential applications of the membrane in water softening. Therefore, the effect of MgCl2 concentration on the separation performance was evaluated (Fig. 6c). The decreased water flux is due to the increased osmotic pressure with the increase of MgCl2 concentration, which reduced the driving force. Meanwhile, the increased MgCl2 concentrations resulted in high Cl− ions concentrations and aggravated the electrostatic shielding effect of Cl− ions toward the positive membrane surface, which was responsible for the decreased MgCl2 rejections of NF-CQD 10 [54]. Overall, NF-CQD 10 exhibited high MgCl2 removal at different concentrations, indicating its potential application in water softening.

Considering the high water flux and low salt rejection exhibited by NF-CQD 1, the potential of NF-CQD 1 for the treatment and reuse of dyeing wastewater was explored. As shown in Fig. 7 a, except for low salt rejection, the rejections for MYB, CR, and VBB are 98.96 %, 99.44 %, and 99.95 %, respectively. The high dye rejection and low salt rejection, in addition to the high water flux, enable NF-CQD 1 to efficiently realize dye/salt separation. To further evaluate the selective separation of the dye/salt mixture by NF-CQD 1, binary component filtration was conducted by filtrating simulated saline textile effluent containing 0.1 g/L CR and different concentrations of NaCl. As shown in Fig. 7b, the water flux and rejection of CR and NaCl gradually decrease with increasing NaCl concentration. However, even though the salt concentration of NaCl was up to 20 g/L, high dye rejection could be maintained; the rejection of CR was 97.33 %, while the NaCl rejection was 4.0 %. A large amount of NaCl offsets the electrical repulsion interactions between the membrane surface and solute, enhancing the extent of the concentration polarization. As a result, the attenuated membrane performance was observed [55,56].

Moreover, the MYB/NaCl separation performance was also provided (Fig. S8), and the water permeability, rejections to dye and salt, and dye/salt separation factor were summarized in Table S1. A more comprehensive comparison of NF-CQD 1 with state-of-the-art NF membranes showed that NF-CQD 1 possessed good water permeability and excellent dye/salt selective separation. Besides, NF-CQD 1 maintained stable water flux (∼57 L m−2h−1) and rejections to CR (∼99 %) and NaCl (∼15 %) in the continuous 48 h filtration process, manifesting the good stability of NF-CQD 1 (Fig. S9a), and no thick dye aggregation layer was observed after the separation (Fig. S9b). Therefore, NF-CQD 1 would exhibit excellent separation performance in textile wastewater treatment by utilizing the unique properties of l-CQDs as membrane materials.

When dyed directly, a large amount of NaCl is added to reduce the electrostatic repulsion between the dyes and fibers and to therefore improve the dye uptake. The direct reuse of NaCl from saline textile effluents in fabric dyeing is generally considered to be a profitable and feasible method in the textile industry. The potential of NF-CQD 1 for NaCl reuse was investigated. The collected permeate solution (100 mL) was utilized for dyeing the cotton fabric, and the color differences in the two dyeing fabrics were characterized (Fig. 7c). Accordingly, the relatively low difference values of K/S, L*, and a* indicate that a negligible difference exists between the fabrics dyed using the two effluents. The higher difference in b* indicates that the cotton fabrics dyed with reused water exhibited more greenness. Therefore, the direct reuse of NaCl from the saline textile effluent in the dyeing process was achieved, indicating the potential application of NF-CQD 1 in textile wastewater treatment.

In this study, charge- and pore-size-tunable NF membranes were successfully fabricated using l-CQDs as the aqueous monomer for interfacial polymerization. The simultaneous transformation of the surface charge from negative to positive and reduction of the pore size of the separation layers was achieved by tailoring the l-CQD concentration. When the l-CQD concentration was 0.1 wt%, the resultant loose NF membrane achieved excellent dye/salt fractionation performance even at 20 g/L NaCl concentration. The direct reuse of NaCl from highly saline dyeing effluent in cotton fabric dyeing was confirmed, indicating its potential application prospects in the treatment of saline textile effluents. Near-neutral and positive surfaces were achieved when the l-CQD concentration increased to 0.8 and 1.0 wt%, respectively. The dense and positively charged separation layers endowed the NF membrane with high rejections of 98.77 %, 99.06 %, and 99.0 % for MgCl2, CuCl2, and CaCl2, respectively. These results indicate that l-CQD-based NF membranes with simultaneously tailorable pore sizes and surface charges have great potential for application in the effective modulation of separation performance.

Yi-Fang Mi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Yi-Han Huang: Investigation, Validation. Shu-Heng He: Investigation, Validation. Rong Ma: Review & Editing. Yi-Ding Meng: Review & Editing. Zhi-Hai Cao: Supervision. All authors read and revised the paper. There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21908200), the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LY22B060011), and Zhejiang Provincial Science and Technology Innovation Program (New Young Talent Program) for College Students (2022R406A005).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2023.123870.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
Characterizations of pore size distribution of membranes; Synthesis and characterizations of L-CQDs; Cross-section images, membrane hydraulic resistance, solute/water selectivity, effect of NaCl concentrations on the separation performance, long-term stability, and performance comparisons could be found in the Supplementary Materials.
